summary: Mithaq is the primordial covenant of Qur'an 7:172, in which God is described as drawing forth the descendants of Adam before creation and making them testify to His lordship with the question 'Am I not your Lord?' (a-lastu bi-rabbikum), to which they answered 'Yes'. The episode, called by the Sufis the Day of Alast, is treated in theological anthropology as the ground of the innate human recognition of God, and in Sufism as the state the mystic seeks to recover.
description:
Mithaq names the covenant that Qur'an 7:172 describes as taking place before earthly life, when God brought forth from the loins of the children of Adam their descendants and made them bear witness against themselves: 'Am I not your Lord?' (a-lastu bi-rabbikum), and they said 'Yes, we testify.' From the question a-lastu the episode is known as the Day of Alast (yawm-i alast) or the Day of the Covenant (yawm al-mithaq). In the broader theological reading the covenant grounds an innate human acknowledgment of God, connected in the exegetical tradition to the disposition (fitra) upon which humankind is said to be created, so that revelation is understood as a reminder of a testimony already given and human responsibility as rooted in a prior consent.

In Sufism (tasawwuf) the covenant became a central image of the soul's origin and destiny. Early Sufi Qur'an commentary, associated in the formative period with figures such as Sahl al-Tustari (d. 283/896), read a-lastu as the moment of the soul's primordial nearness to God and its witnessing of Him, a state from which earthly existence is a separation and to which the path is a return. The theme is prominent in the letters (Rasa'il) of al-Junayd al-Baghdadi (d. c. 910), whose 'sober' (sahw) doctrine treats the goal of the path as a recovery of the condition the soul knew at the covenant: in this framing the passing away of the self (fana') is understood as a return to the state 'as one was before one was', that is, the pre-existent witnessing of the mithaq. Classical manuals such as al-Qushayri's Risala treat the covenant among the foundations of the Sufi understanding of the relation between God and the soul. This entry describes the covenant as the tradition and its scholars present it and reports the differing emphases (theological-anthropological and mystical) without adjudicating between exegetical readings.
editorial_notes: Scope: mithaq as the primordial covenant of Q 7:172 (the Day of Alast), in both its broader theological-anthropological register (innate recognition of God, connection to fitra) and its Sufi register (the soul's pre-eternal witnessing and the path as return), with particular attention to al-Junayd's 'sober' reading in which fana' is a return to the covenantal state. The 'return to the covenantal state' phrasing (the soul's condition as it was at, or before, its coming into being, when it witnessed the covenant) is attributed to the al-Junayd tradition and its scholarly interpreters, not asserted as the sole correct reading, per neutrality principles #2 and #4. The connection to fitra is noted as an exegetical linkage found in the tradition, not adjudicated. Relations: related_to al-junayd, fana, tawhid (al-junayd and fana have entries; tawhid exists in the dataset). Sources triangulate three lenses per neutrality principle #8: (a) a modern Western academic monograph, Bowering's study of Sahl al-Tustari's Qur'anic hermeneutics (de Gruyter 1980), which treats the primordial covenant and the soul's pre-existence in early Sufism at length; (b) the tradition's own classical primary manual, al-Qushayri's Risala, together with al-Junayd's Rasa'il in Abdel-Kader's critical edition/translation, which develop the covenant theme directly; and (c) a contemporary Muslim scholar (Nasr, Garden of Truth). All were web-verified as existing works treating this material; none was read line-by-line in this pass, so confidence is medium. The al-Junayd Rasa'il (Abdel-Kader) is included as a corroborating primary source because al-Junayd's covenant doctrine is load-bearing for the Sufi register described here.

relations.note: The covenant (mithaq) is a central theme of al-Junayd's Rasa'il; his 'sober' doctrine reads the goal of the path as a return to the state the soul knew at the covenant.
In al-Junayd's framing, the passing away of the self (fana') is understood as a return to the pre-existent witnessing of the covenant, the state 'as one was before one was'.
The covenant is read as the primordial affirmation of God's lordship and oneness (tawhid); the exegetical and mystical readings of this linkage differ and are not adjudicated here.

historical_period.note: The covenant is grounded in Qur'an 7:172 (Meccan revelation, 7th c. CE); its sustained mystical and theological elaboration belongs to the formative Sufism and Qur'an commentary of the 3rd/9th century onward (al-Tustari, al-Junayd) and the manuals of the 5th/11th century (al-Qushayri). Start year marks the Qur'anic anchor; the concept as a technical theme is later.

sources.citation: Bowering, Gerhard. The Mystical Vision of Existence in Classical Islam: The Qur'anic Hermeneutics of the Sufi Sahl at-Tustari (d. 283/896). Studien zur Sprache, Geschichte und Kultur des Islamischen Orients, Neue Folge 9. Berlin and New York: Walter de Gruyter, 1980. Modern academic monograph treating the primordial covenant (mithaq) and the pre-existence of souls in early Sufi Qur'an interpretation.
sources.license: Proprietary, cited for reference; text not reproduced.

sources.citation: al-Qushayri, Abu 'l-Qasim. al-Risala al-Qushayriyya fi 'ilm al-tasawwuf (5th/11th c.), trans. Alexander D. Knysh as Al-Qushayri's Epistle on Sufism. Reading: Garnet Publishing, 2007. Classical Sufi manual treating the covenant (mithaq) and the relation between God and the soul among the foundations of the path.
sources.license: Primary source in scholarly translation; cited for reference, relevant passages not read line-by-line.

sources.citation: Abdel-Kader, Ali Hassan. The Life, Personality and Writings of al-Junayd: A Study of a Third/Ninth Century Mystic, with an Edition and Translation of his Writings. E. J. W. Gibb Memorial Series, New Series XII. London: Luzac, 1962. Primary source: critical edition and translation of al-Junayd's Rasa'il, which develop the theme of the primordial covenant (mithaq).
sources.license: Proprietary edition/translation of a classical text; cited for reference (relevant passages not read line-by-line).

sources.citation: Nasr, Seyyed Hossein. The Garden of Truth: The Vision and Promise of Sufism, Islam's Mystical Tradition. New York: HarperOne, 2007. A contemporary Muslim scholar's exposition of Sufism, treating the primordial covenant and the soul's origin and return; added as the modern-Muslim lens. Relevant passages not read line-by-line.
